# LeanCTX

> The Cognitive Filter for AI Engineering. A single Rust binary that reduces LLM token consumption by up to 99% per operation through AST-aware compression, shell output compression, and communication protocols.

## What is LeanCTX?

LeanCTX (lean-ctx) is an open-source compression system for AI coding tools. It sits between your AI tool (Cursor, Claude Code, GitHub Copilot, etc.) and the LLM, compressing context before it reaches the model. This saves tokens, reduces costs, and extends API rate limits.

## Three Compression Layers

1. **MCP Server**: 25 compression tools replace built-in file reads, shell commands, and code search. Uses tree-sitter AST parsing across 18 programming languages. Seven read modes: full, map, signatures, diff, aggressive, entropy, lines:N-M.
2. **Shell Hook**: Intercepts 90+ CLI command patterns (git, npm, cargo, docker, kubectl, pip, go, tsc) and compresses output by 60-90%.
3. **Communication Protocols**: CEP (Cognitive Efficiency Protocol), CCP (Context Continuity Protocol), and TDD (Token Dense Dialect) teach the AI to respond more efficiently, saving 8-25% additional tokens.

## Scientific Intelligence Layer

The compression engine is powered by six research-backed algorithms:

1. **Predictive Surprise** — BPE cross-entropy scoring. Keeps tokens that surprise a language model; discards predictable boilerplate.
2. **Spectral Relevance** — PageRank + heat diffusion on the dependency graph. Propagates importance scores through code relationships.
3. **Boltzmann Allocation** — Statistical mechanics for token budgets. Distributes context capacity based on information temperature.
4. **Semantic Chunking** — Attention bridges identify natural break points in code. Preserves coherent logical units, not arbitrary line counts.
5. **MMR Deduplication** — Jaccard similarity across cached files. Eliminates redundant patterns without losing unique information.
6. **BPE Optimization** — Token-aligned encoding. Restructures output to minimize actual BPE token count, not just character count.

## Key Facts

- License: MIT
- Language: Rust
- Install: `cargo install lean-ctx` or `curl -fsSL https://leanctx.com/install.sh | sh`
- Setup: `lean-ctx init` (shell hook) + `lean-ctx setup` (MCP for AI tools)
- Supported tools: Cursor, GitHub Copilot, Claude Code, Windsurf, Cline, OpenAI Codex, Gemini CLI, Antigravity, Pi, Zed, Continue, OpenCode, Aider, OpenClaw
- Repository: https://github.com/yvgude/lean-ctx
- Crate: https://crates.io/crates/lean-ctx
- Website: https://leanctx.com

## Pages

- [How It Works](https://leanctx.com/how-it-works/): Data flow, compression pipeline, quality preservation
- [MCP Server](https://leanctx.com/mcp-server/): 25 compression tools documentation
- [Shell Hook](https://leanctx.com/shell-hook/): 90+ command patterns with before/after examples
- [Compatibility](https://leanctx.com/compatibility/): All supported AI tools with setup commands
- [Benchmark](https://leanctx.com/benchmark/): Token savings measurement and verification
- [CLI Reference](https://leanctx.com/cli/): Terminal commands for humans
- [Protocols](https://leanctx.com/protocols/): CEP, CCP, and TDD communication protocols
- [Getting Started](https://leanctx.com/docs/getting-started/): Installation and setup guide
- [Configuration](https://leanctx.com/docs/configuration/): config.toml and environment variables
- [Tool API Reference](https://leanctx.com/docs/tools/): All 25 MCP tools documented
- [Intelligence Layer](https://leanctx.com/docs/intelligence-layer/): 6 scientific compression algorithms explained
- [Tree-sitter Engine](https://leanctx.com/docs/tree-sitter/): AST parsing for 18 languages
- [Quick Reference](https://leanctx.com/docs/quick-reference/): Daily workflow cheat sheet
